figures.caption: Fig. 13: A comparison of the original DYNAMOS system and our proof-of-concept FL simulation system.
Fig. 4: Outbound exclusion, where C2 prevents its model from being incorporated into C3's global model.
Fig. 1: Full exclusion: client C1 fully removes itself from FL.
TABLE I: Overview of Disagreement Types
Fig. 2: Partial data exclusion, where C1 withholds a subset of its local data but continues to participate in model aggregation.
Fig. 3: Inbound exclusion, where C1 requires a personalized global model (GM1) that omits C2's contribution.
Fig. 15: Model performance for Scenario 1 (S1) on both datasets. Both tracks demonstrate stable learning, indicating the resolution mechanism does not impede model training.
Fig. 5: Bidirectional exclusion (one excludes one), establishing mutual isolation between C1 and C2.
Fig. 8: Cross-contamination in the naive approach. The influence of the excluded client (C3) leaks back to C1 via the intermediary client C2 in subsequent rounds.
Fig. 6: Multiple exclusion types disagreement scenario with temporal modifiers for duration (clock) and depth (deep icon).
Fig. 7: The naive resolution strategy. C1 inbound excludes C3, receiving a personalized model (GM1). However, intermediary clients like C2 still aggregate all updates.
Fig. 12: Fairness in the robust approach. Through background participation, C1's contribution to C3's track (GM2) is generated under fair and consistent conditions.
Fig. 11: Isolation in the robust approach. Updates are trackspecific, preventing the influence of an excluded client (C3) from propagating across tracks.
Fig. 9: Unfairness in the naive approach. C1 contributes to C3's model, but its updates are based on a personalized model that excluded C3, creating an inequitable aggregation.
Fig. 10: The robust multi-track approach. Two separate and isolated tracks (GM1 and GM2) are created to serve the different disagreement requirements of the clients.
